citation: Oleksy Ł, Mika A, Kuchciak M, Bril G, Kielnar R, Adamska O, Wolański P, Deszczyński M. Does Restricted Ankle Joint Mobility Influence Hamstring Muscle Strength, Work and Power in Football Players after ACL Reconstruction and Non-Injured Players?. J. Clin. Med. 2023;12(19):6330. https://doi.org/10.3390/jcm12196330
abstract: This study investigated how ankle dorsiflexion ROM limitation affects hamstring muscle Peak Torque/BW (%), Average Power (W), and Total Work (J) in 47 professional football players divided into a post-ACL reconstruction group (n=24) and an uninjured group (n=23). Players were sub-classified by the Weight-Bearing Lunge Test (WBLT) into normal (≥12 cm) and restricted (<12 cm) ankle dorsiflexion subgroups, and concentric isokinetic testing at 60°/s was performed on both limbs. Results showed that ankle dorsiflexion restriction had no significant effect on hamstring strength variables within either group, with poor and non-significant correlations between WBLT and all strength measures. AUC values below 0.5 across all variables confirmed that ankle dorsiflexion ROM has no diagnostic accuracy for hamstring muscle strength.
tldr: Ankle dorsiflexion restriction does not weaken hamstring muscles in football players, whether or not they have undergone ACL reconstruction. Post-ACL reconstruction players showed significantly lower hamstring Peak Torque/BW and Average Power compared to uninjured players, but within both groups, restricted ankle mobility did not further impair hamstring strength, power, or total work. Coaches and sports scientists should not assume limited ankle dorsiflexion is a primary driver of hamstring weakness; prevention programs should target a broad spectrum of physical techniques beyond ankle mobility interventions.
methods: Cross-sectional study of 47 professional football players (Group 1: n=24 post-ACL reconstruction 2-3 years prior, cleared for RTS; Group 2: n=23 injury-free for 3 years; mean age ~22.6 years). Ankle dorsiflexion was measured with the WBLT (cut-off 12 cm). Hamstring Peak Torque/BW (%), Average Power (W), and Total Work (J) were assessed via concentric isokinetic dynamometry (Biodex System 4) at 60°/s, 10 repetitions per leg. MANOVA, Pearson correlations, and ROC/AUC analyses were used.
findings: Post-ACL players had significantly lower Peak Torque/BW (Group 1 injured leg: 121 ± ~25% vs Group 2 right leg: 164 ± ~30%, p<0.006) and Average Power compared to uninjured players. Within both groups, no significant differences were found between normal and restricted ankle dorsiflexion subgroups for any strength variable (all p>0.05). Pearson correlations between WBLT and all strength variables were poor (r range: -0.18 to 0.26, all non-significant). AUC values ranged from 0.40 to 0.65, all near or below 0.5, indicating no diagnostic utility.
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. Longitudinal monitoring of both muscle strength and ankle dorsiflexion ROM over a season would provide more robust conclusions. The sample was limited to professional regional-level players, limiting generalizability to elite or other competitive levels.
practicalImplications: Football programs should not rely solely on ankle dorsiflexion assessments to identify athletes at risk for hamstring weakness. Post-ACL reconstruction players exhibit persistent bilateral hamstring strength deficits that require targeted rehabilitation regardless of ankle mobility status. Injury prevention programming should include a broad spectrum of physical training techniques addressing multiple risk factors beyond ankle joint mobility.
athleteDev: Hamstring strength development programs for football players should be prioritized independently of ankle dorsiflexion status. Athletes returning from ACL reconstruction demonstrate lasting hamstring strength deficits bilaterally, reinforcing the need for comprehensive lower-limb conditioning protocols throughout development. Screening should include isokinetic hamstring strength testing in addition to ankle mobility assessments.
rtp: Football players cleared for RTS after ACL reconstruction continue to show significantly reduced hamstring Peak Torque/BW and Average Power compared to uninjured peers, indicating that RTS clearance does not equate to full hamstring strength recovery. RTP protocols should include objective hamstring strength benchmarks and should not be conflated with ankle mobility restoration. Bilateral hamstring strength deficits persisting 2-3 years post-reconstruction highlight the need for extended post-clearance monitoring.

citation: Oleksy Ł, Mika A, Kuchciak M, Bril G, Kielnar R, Adamska O, Wolański P, Deszczyński M. citation: Silva DS, Boullosa D, Pereira EVM, Alves MDJ, Fernandes MSS, Aidar FJ, dos Santos LF, de Souza RF. Does the sequence of plyometric and dynamic stretching exercises influence subsequent sprint performance? A randomized crossover intervention study. Biol Sport. 2024;41(2):13–18. https://doi.org/10.5114/biolsport.2024.129485
abstract: This randomized crossover study examined the acute effects of exercise sequence — dynamic stretching followed by drop jumps (DS+DJ) versus drop jumps followed by dynamic stretching (DJ+DS) — on sprint performance in 13 competitive male track-and-field athletes (sprinters and jumpers). Athletes completed a standardized warm-up and then one of three conditions (DS+DJ, DJ+DS, or control) before performing 40-m sprints analyzed in 0–20 m and 20–40 m phases. The DS+DJ sequence improved final acceleration (20–40 m) speed compared to baseline, while DJ+DS worsened initial acceleration (0–20 m), and neither sequence improved overall 40-m performance.
tldr: For sports performance coaches using warm-up potentiation strategies, the order of dynamic stretching and plyometric exercises matters: performing dynamic stretching before drop jumps (DS+DJ) improved final sprint acceleration (20–40 m) by approximately 0.12 m/s, while reversing the order (DJ+DS) actually impaired initial acceleration. Athletes with lower baseline lower-limb power (lower CMJ height) benefited the most from the DS+DJ sequence, suggesting this strategy is especially valuable for less powerful athletes. This simple, equipment-free protocol is directly applicable in practice and competitive settings.
methods: Randomized crossover design with 13 competitive male sprint and jump athletes (19±2 years; CMJ height 39.0±3.0 cm; 100-m PB 11.4±0.6 s). Five sessions over 3 weeks: 2 familiarization, 3 experimental (72 hours apart). Conditions: DS+DJ (2 dynamic stretches × 15 reps/leg + 3 drop jumps at optimal height), DJ+DS (reverse order), and control. Sprint performance assessed via MySprint app at 240 fps over 40 m with split times at 0–20 m and 20–40 m. CMJ assessed via Chronojump contact mat. Two-way repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni post-hoc; Pearson correlation between CMJ and PAPE response.
findings: No significant effect of any condition on overall 40-m sprint performance. The DS+DJ condition significantly improved 20–40 m velocity vs. baseline (8.91±0.35 vs. 8.79±0.43 m/s; p=0.015). The DJ+DS condition significantly worsened 0–20 m velocity vs. baseline (6.22±0.26 vs. 6.26±0.25 m/s; p=0.002). A significant negative correlation was found between CMJ height and the percentage improvement in final acceleration in the DS+DJ condition (r=-0.741; p=0.004), indicating athletes with lower power benefited most.
limitations: Small sample size (n=13 after exclusions from an initial 16). All participants were competitive track-and-field athletes (sprinters and jumpers), limiting generalizability to team sport athletes. Only two dynamic stretching exercises and three drop jumps were used; results may differ with different exercise volumes or types. The study did not measure neuromuscular mechanisms (EMG, force-velocity characteristics) to explain the observed sequence effects.
practicalImplications: Football coaches and sports performance staff can use DS followed by DJ (DS+DJ) as a simple, equipment-free warm-up potentiation strategy to enhance final-phase sprint acceleration. This is particularly relevant for skill positions requiring top-end speed after an initial acceleration phase. Athletes with lower lower-limb power levels benefit most, making this strategy valuable for developing players. The reverse sequence (DJ then DS) should be avoided as it can impair initial acceleration, which is critical in football contexts.
athleteDev: Implementing DS+DJ sequencing in warm-up protocols can serve as a development tool for football skill positions (WR, DB, RB) requiring explosive acceleration in the 20–40 m range. Athletes with lower CMJ heights — often younger or less trained players — show the greatest performance enhancement, suggesting this PAPE strategy is especially valuable during developmental stages. Coaches should individualize potentiation warm-up strategies based on assessed lower-limb power levels.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play populations. However, the emphasis on exercise sequencing during warm-up may be relevant for athletes returning from lower-limb injuries who are reintegrating sprint-based activities. Caution is warranted: the DJ+DS sequence impaired initial acceleration, which could stress healing tissue in RTP athletes; DS+DJ may represent a safer potentiation strategy for athletes in the later stages of return-to-activity progressions.

citation: Bishay AE, Fitch SW, Iheagwaram CS, Williams KL, Naranjo C, Sills TK, Fitch RW, Pasquina PF, McAllister TW, McCrea MA, Broglio SP, Terry DP, Zuckerman SL; CARE Consortium Investigators. Does Timing of Concussion in Season Affect Incidence and Recovery in Division I Collegiate Football Players? A CARE Consortium Study. J Head Trauma Rehabil. 2026;Vol 00, No 0:1–10. https://doi.org/10.1097/HTR.0000000000001146
abstract: This prospective cohort study from the CARE Consortium investigated whether the timing of concussion within a football season (preseason, early, middle, or late-season) is associated with concussion incidence and recovery outcomes (time to symptom resolution [SR] and return-to-play [RTP]) in 682 Division I collegiate football players across 21 institutions (2014–2020). Concussion incidence was highest during preseason (4.73 per 100 players) compared to all regular-season phases (~2.2 per 100). Early and late-season concussions had significantly longer time to SR compared to preseason (HR=0.582 and 0.634, respectively), and early-season concussions also had a lower hazard of RTP (HR=0.703).
tldr: In NCAA Division I football, preseason carries the highest risk of concussion — nearly double the rate of any regular-season phase — but preseason concussions resolve fastest (median 5.7 days to SR). Concussions sustained during the early and late regular season take significantly longer to resolve (median 6.6 and 6.9 days to SR, respectively), and early-season concussions also prolong return-to-play. Football medical staff should increase concussion surveillance and prevention during preseason, while recognizing that early- and late-season concussions may require extended management windows, possibly due to academic demands, cumulative fatigue, or competitive pressures.
methods: Prospective cohort study using the CARE Consortium multi-institution database (30 universities, 2014–2020). Final sample: 682 Division I football players across 21 institutions who sustained concussions during preseason or regular-season. Season phases were defined as preseason (August), early (Sep 1–29), middle (Sep 30–Oct 28), and late-season (Oct 29–Nov 27) on a per-team basis. Concussion incidence expressed per 100 players. Time to SR and RTP analyzed with Kaplan-Meier survival curves, log-rank tests, and Cox proportional hazards regression with covariates including age, SCAT3 TSS, LOC, amnesia, ADHD, and psychological disorders.
findings: Preseason incidence: 4.73/100 players (41.9% of concussions); early: 2.19, middle: 2.31, late: 2.17/100 (χ²=104.6, p<0.001). Kaplan-Meier showed significant SR differences (log-rank p=0.012): median SR was preseason 5.7 days, early 6.6, middle 4.8, late 6.9 days. Cox regression: early-season concussions had 41.8% reduced SR hazard (HR=0.582, p<0.001); late-season had 35.6% reduced SR hazard (HR=0.634, p=0.002) vs preseason. No overall RTP differences (log-rank p=0.300), but early-season concussions had 29.7% reduced RTP hazard (HR=0.703, p=0.040) vs preseason. Higher SCAT3 TSS and delayed SCAT3 testing also predicted longer SR and RTP.
limitations: Arbitrary season phase cutoffs limit direct comparisons with studies using different definitions. Lack of exposure data prevents precise incidence rate calculations. Observational design cannot establish causality or distinguish mechanisms (e.g., training load, cumulative head impacts, academic demands). Sample limited to Division I football; results may not generalize to other levels or sports. Concussion management protocols evolved over the 2014–2020 study period, potentially influencing reported outcomes.
practicalImplications: Football programs should implement heightened concussion surveillance, prevention protocols, and head impact monitoring during preseason — the highest-risk period — including attention to conditioning status, practice intensity, and helmet-fit protocols. Athletic trainers should be aware that early- and late-season concussions may require longer management timelines, potentially due to academic load, cumulative fatigue, and playoff pressure. Symptom severity (SCAT3 TSS) at 24 hours post-injury remains a key prognostic indicator regardless of season phase.
athleteDev: Preseason represents the highest concussion incidence period for collegiate football players, likely linked to elevated head impact exposure, deconditioning from the off-season, and intense competition for roster spots. Programs should prioritize preseason neuromuscular conditioning, proper tackling technique, and gradual ramp-up of contact to reduce early-season concussion burden. Player wellness and concussion literacy education at the start of each season phase is essential.
rtp: Timing of concussion within the season significantly affects RTP trajectory: early-season concussions showed a 30% lower hazard of RTP compared to preseason, with median RTP ranging from 10.7 to 12.7 days across phases. Medical staff should not expect uniform RTP timelines across the season; late-season concussions in particular may be complicated by cumulative fatigue and playoff stakes. Post-injury SCAT3 symptom severity is the strongest clinical predictor of prolonged RTP and should anchor all return-to-play decision-making.

citation: Cairns JT, Kolstad AT, Galarneau J-M, van den Berg CA, Jordan MJ, Schneider KJ, Pasanen K, Emery CA. Down, set, record: Assessing warm-up structure and use of neuromuscular training exercises in Canadian adolescent tackle football. International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching. 2025;1–11. https://doi.org/10.1177/17479541251373073
abstract: This cross-sectional video-analysis study examined warm-up structure and neuromuscular training (NMT) component use across 46 warm-up sessions (23 practice, 23 game) from nine adolescent (ages 14–17) tackle football teams in Calgary, Canada during a nine-week 2022 season. Researchers coded warm-up footage for duration and exercise type across NMT components (aerobic, strength, agility, balance/coordination, head-and-neck control) and non-NMT components (dynamic and static stretching). Teams spent a median of 456.9 seconds total in warm-up with 266.7 seconds actively exercising, and 80% of exercises were aerobic (51%) or dynamic stretching (29%), while NMT-specific components (strength 9%, agility 3%, balance/coordination 1%, head-and-neck control 1%) were substantially underutilized.
tldr: Canadian adolescent tackle football coaches are not implementing evidence-based neuromuscular training (NMT) warm-up programs, which have been shown to reduce musculoskeletal injuries by >35% and concussions by 59% in adolescent sports. Warm-ups are dominated by aerobic running and dynamic stretching, while strength, agility, balance/coordination, and head-and-neck control exercises — critical NMT components — collectively account for less than 10% of all exercises. Head-and-neck control exercises, which are especially important for concussion prevention in a collision sport, were used by only one team. Football programs should restructure warm-ups to include all five NMT components and extend active exercise time to the recommended 10–15 minutes.
methods: Cross-sectional video-analysis sub-study within the SHRed Concussions research program. Nine tackle football teams (Calgary Spring Football Association, April–June 2022) were filmed across 46 warm-ups (23 practice, 23 game) over a 9-week season. Dartfish software (v10.1) was used to code exercise components and durations. A full factorial mixed effects linear regression model compared warm-up structure by session type (game vs practice) and season period (weeks 1–3, 4–6, 7–9), using restricted maximum likelihood estimation and Kenward-Roger degrees of freedom.
findings: Median total warm-up time was 456.9 s (25th–75th percentile: 276.8–532.3 s); median active exercise time was 266.7 s (193.5–326.9 s). Aerobic exercises comprised 51% (283/550) and dynamic stretching 29% (158/550) of all exercises. NMT components: strength 9% (51 exercises), agility 3% (15), balance/coordination 1% (3), head-and-neck control 1% (6). No significant differences in warm-up structure between game and practice sessions. The only statistically significant seasonal change was that aerobic exercise time was 42.2 s longer in the middle season period (weeks 4–6) than the beginning (p=0.02). Only one team consistently used head-and-neck control exercises.
limitations: Minimum of 4 warm-ups filmed per team due to feasibility constraints limited precision of estimates. Presence of the research team may have altered coach behavior. Only team-structured warm-ups were examined, excluding individual player warm-ups. Exercise fidelity was not assessed. Some exercises were classified into a single component category despite potential overlap. Sample limited to Calgary adolescent male teams, limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: Tackle football programs must substantially restructure their warm-up protocols to incorporate all NMT components — particularly strength, agility, balance/coordination, and head-and-neck control — which are currently near-absent. Coaches should target 10–15 minutes of active NMT-based warm-up per session, 2–3 times per week, consistent with dose-response evidence. Head-and-neck control exercises should be standard given football's second-highest sport concussion rate. The video-analysis methodology used in this study provides a practical tool for coaching staff to audit their own warm-up practices.
athleteDev: Adolescent tackle football players (ages 14–17) are not receiving evidence-based NMT warm-up preparation that develops strength, agility, balance/coordination, and head-and-neck control — all critical athletic attributes in a collision sport. Coaches should replace excess aerobic running time with structured NMT exercises targeting these components. Implementation of a tackle football-specific NMT program (analogous to FIFA 11+ or Rugby Activate) would provide a structured framework for coaches without specialized strength and conditioning knowledge.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play populations. However, the near-absence of head-and-neck control and neuromuscular strength exercises in current warm-up practice has direct implications for concussion and musculoskeletal injury prevention, both of which affect RTP timelines. Programs that implement NMT warm-ups with head-and-neck control components and strength exercises may reduce primary concussion incidence and lower-limb injury rates, thereby reducing the frequency of RTP scenarios that football medical staff must manage.
